narrative:
As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, LSAT score, family background, and other details to forecast your bar performance. For you specifically, it calculated a 69.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is high enough that the admissions team decided to reject your application to minimize the risk of poor outcomes for both you and the school. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the decision.

The most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your peers that year—this strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as lower rankings like yours signal challenges in sustaining strong performance over time. Next, your LSAT score of 37, which is only slightly above the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing solid but not standout test-taking ability compared to typical applicants. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 1 again indicated bottom-tier grades early on, reinforcing the model's concern about foundational academic struggles and nudging the outcome further toward failure.

Overall, your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 falls well below the typical 3.21 average, and while your middle family income bracket is common, these elements compounded the early grade concerns in the model's eyes—especially since most admitted students show stronger decile progress. Full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority of students (over 92%), so that didn't sway things much. Compared to peers who typically get approved, your consistently low early deciles stand out as the biggest red flags, outweighing your decent LSAT.

To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it significantly above average, while addressing those early undergraduate grades—perhaps through a post-baccalaureate program or targeted tutoring to demonstrate improvement. Building a narrative of academic turnaround in a new application, maybe with letters highlighting your resilience as a Black man navigating these challenges, could shift future predictions. Reapply after showing concrete progress; many students in similar spots succeed by targeting schools with holistic reviews that value potential beyond numbers.
